title: The Jazzaguy

jokes.text: Um, Alexis, do you mind telling your phone to fuck off?
jokes.setup: An unseen phone notification is disrupting the scene
jokes.punchline: David's hilariously formal phrasing — 'do you mind telling your phone to fuck off' — treats an inanimate object as a social intruder
jokes.explanation: The joke works because of David's character voice — the overly polite framing around a blunt obscenity is quintessentially him. It's a strong cold-open character establishment beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, could you please turn off your phone? The constant buzzing is making me feel like I'm trapped in some sort of dystopian nightmare where technology has replaced human decency.

jokes.text: It's an online social hub for rural singles, if you must know, David.
jokes.setup: David asks what 'Bumpkin' is, clearly recognizing it as a dating app
jokes.punchline: Alexis rebrands it with pretentious language to avoid admitting she's on a dating app for rural people
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in character for Alexis — she euphemizes to preserve dignity. 'Online social hub for rural singles' is a beautifully specific piece of self-delusion.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is this a dating app called 'Bumpkin'?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's 'Bumpkiné.' I added an accent.

jokes.text: Well, why does it say Alexis you have a new match? / Research. I was testing the water for her, David. Poor thing's clueless.
jokes.setup: Alexis claims she signed Twyla up for the dating app as a favor
jokes.punchline: David catches that the app says Alexis herself has a new match, exposing her lie immediately
jokes.explanation: Classic quick-catch lie exposure. Alexis's justification ('testing the water') followed immediately by 'Poor thing's clueless' — which is the real Alexis — is a tidy double-layer joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, what are you doing on a dating app?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not on a dating app, David. I'm just... helping Twyla navigate it. She's so clueless about these things.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's your profile picture.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, yes, because I need to show her what a good profile looks like. It's called being a good friend.

jokes.text: Why is he shirtless with an armful of puppies? / Oh my god, we matched? / His nipples are out.
jokes.setup: David sees Miguel's profile photo and reacts with increasing disbelief
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'shirtless with puppies' → 'we matched?' → 'his nipples are out' builds absurdly, with David fixated on the nipples as the defining detail
jokes.explanation: Three-beat escalation that works on every level — the puppies are a classic 'try-hard attractive guy' trope, Alexis's astonishment is endearing, and David's laser focus on the nipples as the real offense is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: His dating profile photo is him holding a puppy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A puppy? Why would he use a photo with a puppy?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, it's weird. But also, his nipple is showing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's the real problem here.

jokes.text: Ted can't know about this, David. Miguel's the only other vet in town, and they have this like dumb rivalry.
jokes.setup: Alexis is worried about Ted finding out about the match
jokes.punchline: The justification is framed as concern for Ted's feelings, but the real reveal is that Alexis has detailed knowledge of local veterinary professional rivalries — which is absurdly specific and self-aware
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the Miguel storyline. The 'dumb rivalry' self-awareness is a small but effective character beat for Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you've dated everyone in this town?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Not everyone, David. I have standards.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Your standards are a pulse and a credit card.

jokes.text: When has that ever stopped you? I remember that summer you dated all 3 Hanson brothers.
jokes.setup: Alexis invokes her concern for Ted as a reason not to pursue Miguel
jokes.punchline: David recalls the specific, absurd detail that Alexis once dated all three Hanson brothers — 'MMMBop' era band — as evidence she has no moral high ground
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional Schitt's Creek writing. The Hanson brothers callback deepens Alexis's absurd celebrity-adjacent backstory, is delivered with devastating economy by David, and is laugh-out-loud funny because of how specific and unexpected it is. 'All 3' is the killer detail. Qualifies as an 8+ because it's original, character-specific, economical, and genuinely surprising.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This is completely different. Those other times I was just using people for money and status. But this time, I actually care about him.

jokes.text: This is different. I no longer need backstage passes to the Teen Choice Awards.
jokes.setup: David mocks Alexis for dating all three Hanson brothers
jokes.punchline: Alexis's counter isn't a moral defense — it's a practical one: she's outgrown needing Teen Choice Awards access. The implication that she once needed it is the joke.
jokes.explanation: This is the perfect button on the Hanson joke. Alexis doesn't deny the dating — she explains it with a perfectly Alexis-brained logic: it was a transactional arrangement for celebrity access. The specificity of 'Teen Choice Awards' is inspired. It's earned, character-perfect, and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What do you want me to do? I can't help it if I'm attracted to someone. It's not like I have a choice. If a man is attractive, I have to be with him. That's just how it works.

jokes.text: I swiped right, obviously. Like look at him, David. I didn't actually expect we'd match.
jokes.setup: David asks how Alexis matched with Miguel if she didn't mean to
jokes.punchline: She admits she swiped right because he's attractive — the 'research' cover story completely collapses — delivered with total lack of embarrassment
jokes.explanation: The charm is in Alexis's shamelessness. Functional but not exceptional — the joke is somewhat predictable once we know she matched.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's not 'The Gram', David. It's 'Instagram'.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I know that.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, because you literally just called it 'The Gram' three times.

jokes.text: He's the only attractive person on here, David, what did you want me to do? / Well, then go on a date with him.
jokes.setup: Alexis explains why she swiped right on Miguel despite not planning to match
jokes.punchline: David's flat 'Well, then go on a date with him' cuts through all of Alexis's overthinking with maximum efficiency
jokes.explanation: David's deadpan practicality is always funny but this is a fairly standard beat. Works in context.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What did he say?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He said 'Sup?'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay.

jokes.text: I am suffering romantically right now, and there was a minute when I thought I would never have to look at another dating app, and here you are shoving Bumpkins in my face.
jokes.setup: David tries to give Alexis advice on the Miguel situation
jokes.punchline: David derails into his own romantic suffering, making the conversation entirely about himself — 'shoving Bumpkins in my face' is a wonderfully indignant phrase
jokes.explanation: Classic David — pivoting from support to self-pity with complete sincerity. 'Shoving Bumpkins in my face' is an unexpectedly vivid phrase that elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny's working constantly. I barely see him anymore. Do you know what I miss most? My afternoon massage appointments. He used to drive me to them.

jokes.text: It's Bumpkin with a wink emoji.
jokes.setup: David has just called it 'Bumpkins' (plural) in his rant
jokes.punchline: Alexis corrects only the name — not the substance of anything David said — with complete calm and the specific detail of the wink emoji
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on David's rant. The economy is exceptional — four words that reframe the whole exchange as Alexis only caring about brand accuracy. The wink emoji detail is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, Moira, I think if we're asking people to clean up after themselves, we should probably have cleaning supplies. And soap. And towels that don't smell like a wet dog.

jokes.text: He messaged me again. He actually sounds kind of chill, David. / What did he say? / Sup?
jokes.setup: Alexis builds up Miguel's message as evidence he's 'kind of chill'
jokes.punchline: The entire message is 'Sup?' — the most minimal possible communication, making Alexis's reading of it as sophisticated completely absurd
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection/subverted expectation. 'Kind of chill' sets up something substantive; 'Sup?' is the perfect anti-climax. David's 'Okay.' response seals it. Very efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do you know what we spent forty-five minutes on at Jazzagals today? Vocal warm-ups. Forty-five minutes! We were doing lip trills like a deranged horse with a neurological disorder. My lips were vibrating so intensely, I thought I was having a small stroke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And then we moved on to 'mum-mum-mum' sounds. Mum-mum-mum! I didn't pay for years of acting classes to stand in a church basement making baby noises like I'm at a Mommy and Me class.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The woman running it kept saying, 'Really feel it in your diaphragm, Moira.' Well, I'm feeling something, and it's rage.

jokes.text: And I just gave Stevie the day off. / How many days off is Stevie allowed to request?
jokes.setup: Johnny explains he can't make lunch because he has work to do at the motel
jokes.punchline: Moira's question reveals she has no conception of employment norms — 'How many days off is Stevie allowed to request?' treats employee rights as an affront
jokes.explanation: Solid Moira character comedy — her cluelessness about normal working-class life is always rich territory. The oblivious entitlement lands.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you're canceling lunch, that's the third time this month. I've been keeping track. First there was Tuesday when you had that conference call, then Thursday when you claimed to have a migraine, and now today.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, I don't think you need to—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, but I do. I have it all written down here. Canceled lunches, broken promises, instances of neglect. It's very organized. Color-coded, even. You're in the red, Johnny. Literally and figuratively.

jokes.text: It's starting to remind me of Rose Video, when your work consumed you, and I would miss you so. Except now I don't have the jet and the free DVD's to distract me.
jokes.setup: Moira is complaining that Johnny is prioritizing work over their time together
jokes.punchline: The parallel she draws to the old life is poignant — then punctured by the specific absurdity that the compensations she's lost include 'the jet and the free DVDs' as if those are equivalent
jokes.explanation: The humor is in the deflation — free DVDs as a consolation prize is a funny detail that also serves as a gentle commentary on what the Roses have lost.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not depressed. I'm just... conserving energy. For things that matter.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Like... existing.

jokes.text: Oh, there's so much time wasted off top, John. Yap, yap, yap, do, re, mi. A lip buzzing 'til I'm blue in the face!
jokes.setup: Moira mentions she'll attend Jazzagals rehearsal since lunch is canceled
jokes.punchline: Her contempt for the warm-up exercises — rendered as 'yap yap yap, do re mi, lip buzzing' — reveals she considers herself above the process
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical disdain for anything she finds beneath her is well-deployed here. The physical description of 'lip buzzing' is visual and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Because you need to relax, and I need to sit in a hot tub and not talk to you for two hours.

jokes.text: Okay. As long as we're tabulating scores, this will mark the 3rd time you stood me up this week, John.
jokes.setup: Johnny promises to make it up to Moira
jokes.punchline: 'As long as we're tabulating scores' is an unusual Moira-ism that makes the grievance sound like a legal proceeding — then the '3rd time' is a genuine escalation
jokes.explanation: Moira's formal vocabulary applied to domestic complaints is always her best register. 'Tabulating scores' is a lovely Moira-ism.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Look, I know it seems like I'm just being generous with the spa treatments, but the real reason is practical. I got a Groupon.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: But also, I genuinely care about you people.

jokes.text: Turn everything off, please. / Why aren't you dressed? / Because I'm not going. Because I'm too tired. / You've been sleeping for two days. / What's your point?
jokes.setup: Stevie arrives to take David somewhere; he is clearly depressed and in bed
jokes.punchline: 'You've been sleeping for two days' / 'What's your point?' — David's total indifference to this as evidence of a problem is perfectly delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: 'What's your point?' after being told you've slept for two days is a great deadpan line. Very David, very economical.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm too depressed to do anything.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What about a facial?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, when do they have an opening?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wow, depression cured.

jokes.text: I'm finding it hard to believe that you don't have the energy to go to a spa. / I know, I'm scaring even me.
jokes.setup: Stevie challenges David's claim of exhaustion given the destination is a relaxing spa
jokes.punchline: David's self-aware 'I know, I'm scaring even me' is a moment of genuine self-deprecating humor about how far he's fallen
jokes.explanation: Self-aware David is always effective. 'Scaring even me' shows he has just enough self-awareness to recognize his own pathetic state, which is funnier than complete obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, what are my options here? It's not like there's a ton of eligible men in this town.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ray Butley is literally the most eligible bachelor in Schitt's Creek.

jokes.text: Because you're having boyfriend issues, and this is my reluctant attempt at being supportive. / Well, that's very thoughtful of you. / I also have a Groupon, which I was planning on using with Jake, but I thought you might need it more.
jokes.setup: Stevie frames taking David to the spa as a selfless supportive act
jokes.punchline: The reveal that she had a Groupon she was going to use with Jake anyway completely deflates her altruism — and 'I thought you might need it more' is a brilliant backhanded save
jokes.explanation: This is very well-constructed. The setup builds genuine warmth, the Groupon reveal is an earned subversion, and 'I thought you might need it more' works as both practical and backhanded. Stevie's character — pragmatic, unsentimental but genuinely caring — is perfectly captured. Justifies the 8+ rating.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A jazzy gal, she's got that spark, she lights up like a lark, in the dark, with her heart, never apart from the art of the—

dialogue.speaker: Jazzagals
dialogue.line: JAZZ!

jokes.text: What kinda spa is it, and will there be treatments? / You're a monster.
jokes.setup: David has finally agreed to get up and go, seemingly motivated
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the only thing that moved him was spa treatment curiosity — not concern for his wellbeing — earns Stevie's 'You're a monster'
jokes.explanation: Quick, economical button on the 'getting David out of bed' sequence. Stevie's 'You're a monster' is perfectly timed and underplayed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I've freed up some time by delegating more responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I asked Roland to handle the inventory at the store.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I'm doing a great job!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We'll see.

jokes.text: Miguel looks so strong holding all those puppies. / Maybe I should get into online dating? / Oh, I'm not into online dating, I'm just showing David how it works.
jokes.setup: Twyla sees the photo of Miguel on the app
jokes.punchline: Alexis reflexively denies being on the app even as she's using it — the reversal of who the app was supposedly for is now complete
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the opening scene. Alexis now uses 'showing David' as her cover story instead of Twyla, revealing how quickly she cycles through justifications.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, darling, why would you delegate such an important task to Roland? He's hardly the most competent man in town.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, I completely understand your concern, Moira. My own husband can barely tie his shoes without instructions.

jokes.text: It's just that there are like ten guys on here, and three of them are Ray.
jokes.setup: Alexis is scrolling through potential matches on Bumpkin
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Ray — a recurring local character — has THREE separate profiles is unexpected and perfectly absurd
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional one-liner. The specificity ('three of them are Ray'), the economy (nine words), and the absurdity of Ray having multiple profiles on a small-town dating app is genuinely inspired. It rewards knowledge of the show's world without requiring it. Near-perfect joke construction.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Oh wow, the café. How romantic. Nothing says 'I love you' like a grilled cheese and a side of regret.

jokes.text: ♪ Fat spatula ♪
jokes.setup: A quiet beat follows Alexis's Ray revelation; Twyla has been humming
jokes.punchline: The words she's been humming turn out to be 'Fat spatula' — completely random, completely in character for Twyla's dreamy obliviousness
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best moments. Two words, no setup, complete absurdity. 'Fat spatula' is funny as a sound, funny as a concept, and perfectly characterizes Twyla's gentle weirdness. The economy is flawless. Justifies 8+ — it's not a conventional joke but it's a masterclass in character-based absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've had a very robust romantic life. I've been swept off my feet more times than a broom at a Cinderella convention. I've worn white more often than a papal delegate, and I've said 'I do' so many times, the phrase has lost all meaning.

jokes.text: Ah! John, what are you doing here? / Is it David? / Oh no, you can speak freely in front of the gals.
jokes.setup: Johnny surprises Moira at Jazzagals rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Moira's immediate assumption it must be a family emergency ('Is it David?'), followed by her granting permission to 'speak freely in front of the gals' as if this is a wartime briefing, is delightfully theatrical
jokes.explanation: Moira's dramatic register is always reliable. 'Speak freely in front of the gals' is a good character beat — she treats the Jazzagals as her confidantes despite barely tolerating them.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny's always been humble. Selfless, really. He won't sing in the choir because he doesn't want to show up all the other singers with his golden pipes.

jokes.text: Sweet gesture, but was that wise? / No offense, Jocelyn. / I was gonna ask him the same thing.
jokes.setup: Johnny reveals he delegated cleaning to Roland so he could attend rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'No offense, Jocelyn' preemptively acknowledges the insult about Roland, and Jocelyn's cheerful agreement is the real punchline — she also knows Roland is unreliable
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's cheerful agreement that her husband is untrustworthy is a running gag with reliable payoffs. The 'No offense' courtesy is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I wouldn't want to impose, but if you really need me to sing, I suppose I could... no, no, actually I can't sing at all. I have no musical ability whatsoever.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: 🎵 But if the moment called for it, I might just find my voice, and the stars would align, and— 🎵 No, see, this is exactly what I mean. I can't sing!

jokes.text: If that doesn't scream romance. / There was a time when this dusky charmer used to constantly whisk me off to ports unknown.
jokes.setup: Johnny announces he's taking Moira to the café for date night
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's 'if that doesn't scream romance' is dry sarcasm about the café; Moira's response inadvertently confirms it by comparing it wistfully to being whisked to 'ports unknown'
jokes.explanation: 'Dusky charmer' and 'ports unknown' are great Moira-isms. The gap between what they have and what they had is gently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Holy shit, Johnny. You can sing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? No, I was just—

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Don't be modest. That was incredible. You have a real gift.

jokes.text: Oh, my husband, the portrait of self-effacement. / You should all know that this man's voice melted more than a few hearts.
jokes.setup: Johnny politely declines to join the rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical praise — 'portrait of self-effacement,' 'melted more than a few hearts' — is both genuinely affectionate and hilariously over-the-top for the context
jokes.explanation: Moira's vocabulary ('portrait of self-effacement') is a reliable source of amusement. She cannot resist theatrics even in a casual social setting.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Look, I don't know what kind of music you're into, but if you're thinking you're gonna come in here and start playing some soft-rock, yacht-club nonsense, you can forget about it. I'm talking real music. I'm talking Guns N' Roses, AC/DC, maybe some Def Leppard if we're feeling fancy. You got that?

jokes.text: No, no, no! No, no, I... haven't used the old pipes in so long, ♪ impo-o-o-ose ♪
jokes.setup: Johnny repeatedly refuses to sing, protesting he hasn't used his voice in years
jokes.punchline: Mid-sentence, he involuntarily breaks into a beautifully held singing note on the word 'impose' — proving immediately that he can indeed sing
jokes.explanation: This is a standout physical/performance joke. The structure is perfect: three 'no's, the set up of the protest, then the ironic refutation of his own argument by breaking into song. The fact that 'impose' is the word he sings on is the perfect ironic choice. Justifies 8+ — it's the kind of moment that makes audiences gasp and laugh simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, so Jake's out there living his best life, is he? Sleeping around like it's a full-time job, while some of us are actually trying to maintain standards and basic hygiene in our romantic endeavors.

jokes.text: Somebody just nailed their audition.
jokes.setup: Johnny inadvertently demonstrated his singing ability while claiming he can't sing
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's flat, immediate 'Somebody just nailed their audition' is the perfect deadpan button
jokes.explanation: Five words that perfectly cap the 'impose' joke. Ronnie's deadpan delivery is her character's defining trait and this is one of her best moments. Economy is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is... he's your soulmate.

jokes.text: Oh, ha ha. / I've got perfect pitch, so do not mess this up for me.
jokes.setup: Johnny laughs off Ronnie's audition comment; Moira responds
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'I've got perfect pitch, so do not mess this up for me' reveals she's already thinking about how Johnny's participation reflects on HER — and implies she'll be judging him harshly
jokes.explanation: Perfectly encapsulates Moira — she can't help making her husband's return to singing about her own reputation. The 'perfect pitch' claim is delivered with full diva authority.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what's going on with you and Jake?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We're just... we're keeping things casual. He comes over, we hang out, we do some stuff, and then he leaves.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What kind of stuff?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know... stuff. Physical stuff. We're physical. It's very physical.

jokes.text: Oh wow. Is this some kind of sick joke?
jokes.setup: David and Stevie arrive at the spa; David sees the room
jokes.punchline: From context, the room appears to be a honeymoon suite — which is deeply uncomfortable for David given his romantic situation. His 'sick joke' reaction tells us everything about what he's seeing.
jokes.explanation: The reveal works because of David's romantic pain — a honeymoon suite is the worst possible context. His reaction sells the visual gag.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I have a lot of unresolved issues with my parents, and I think I use romantic relationships as a way to avoid dealing with my own emotional unavailability, which is rooted in my fear of abandonment.

jokes.text: Yeah, um, so Jake and I found out that if you tell these resorts that it's your honeymoon, they usually upgrade your room and stuff, and give you free booze.
jokes.setup: David is horrified by the romantic honeymoon suite
jokes.punchline: Stevie casually reveals she and her non-boyfriend Jake routinely commit this small fraud as a lifestyle choice — completely unselfconsciously
jokes.explanation: This deepens our picture of Stevie and Jake's arrangement in a funny way. The casualness with which she describes the honeymoon fraud is the joke — it's clearly a practiced system.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, we need you for this honeymoon scam.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. I'm not doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: But you get a free trip to Mexico.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: When do we leave?

jokes.text: So, Jake and his little pony are goin' on dates and having sexy honeymoon weekends?
jokes.setup: Stevie explains that Jake does sometimes use the honeymoon trick
jokes.punchline: 'Jake and his little pony' — a veiled reference to Jake's apparently notable physical endowment, delivered with prissy outrage by David
jokes.explanation: The euphemism 'little pony' is deliberately contradictory (little = the opposite of what's implied) and the vagueness is funnier than explicitness. David's indignation adds to it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So, Miguel, do you come here often?

dialogue.speaker: Miguel
dialogue.line: I work here.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, of course. Well, I'd love to... come here often too.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, not in a weird way. Just like, as a customer. To buy things. With my mouth.

jokes.text: It's impossible to be in love with Jake, only Jake is in love with Jake. He's just... really good at celebrating my body. So, I'm keepin' him around.
jokes.setup: David suggests Stevie might be falling in love with Jake
jokes.punchline: 'He's just really good at celebrating my body' is an unusual and brilliant way to describe a sexual connection — both euphemistic and oddly specific
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. 'Celebrating my body' is an original, specific, non-vulgar way to describe physical intimacy that is entirely Stevie's voice — she would never say something romantic or clinical. The follow-up 'So, I'm keepin' him around' is the perfectly unsentimental button. Justifies 8+ as genuinely fresh and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Miguel
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny because... nipples are... they're just there, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, why would you say that? Why would you keep talking about nipples?

dialogue.speaker: Miguel
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to normalize the conversation!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, you're doing the opposite of that. You're denormalizing it. You're making it weird.

jokes.text: Ooh. Feeling very raw right now, you know? Um, it's just like a lot of emotional triggers.
jokes.setup: David begins to explain why the honeymoon suite is painful
jokes.punchline: The pause before each fragment — 'Feeling very raw... a lot of emotional triggers' — delivered while surrounded by romantic decor, is funny because David is genuinely suffering and also performing his suffering simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy. David's therapy-speak applied to a genuinely absurd situation. Not the episode's strongest beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Do you have any champagne?

dialogue.speaker: Miguel
dialogue.line: We have sparkling cider. It's très fancy.

jokes.text: So, thank you for saving me from myself because one more day at that motel and I think things would've gotten very dark.
jokes.setup: David reflects on his depressed state in the motel room
jokes.punchline: 'Things would've gotten very dark' — the dramatic implication contrasted with the reality that he'd just been lying in bed — is gentle dark humor
jokes.explanation: The dramatic framing of 'things would've gotten very dark' for what was clearly just extended sleeping is a nice understated joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The irony of someone who dated all the Hanson brothers talking about careful choices

jokes.text: Okay I'm not playing into your fraudulent behaviour. / You still want the free booze, right? / Gimme the ring.
jokes.setup: Stevie produces a prop wedding ring for their fake honeymoon charade; David objects on moral grounds
jokes.punchline: The mention of 'free booze' instantly collapses David's moral stance — he immediately demands the ring
jokes.explanation: Perfect three-beat structure: moral objection → one temptation → instant capitulation. 'Gimme the ring' is a great punchline because it's so sudden and complete. The joke works on character (David's values are fully negotiable) and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you two are just glowing! How long have you been married?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you. We just got married yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: Oh, that's so romantic! You must be so in love. I can just feel it between you two.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yes, we're very much in love.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: This is the most beautiful thing I've ever seen. Let me get you our best champagne on the house!

jokes.text: Oh John, a near-perfect return to form. / Well, professionally speaking there's still only one [singer in the family].
jokes.setup: After singing with the Jazzagals, Johnny is praised
jokes.punchline: Moira's backhanded compliment ('near-perfect') and immediate professional clarification preserves her status as the family's Only Real Talent
jokes.explanation: Moira cannot give a pure compliment — it must always be contextualised in relation to her own superior artistry. 'Professionally speaking there's still only one' is a brilliant qualifier. Highly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, you're going to look ridiculous in a honeymoon hat no matter what. At least this way, you'll look ridiculous on purpose, which is somehow less embarrassing than looking ridiculous by accident.

jokes.text: But I do applaud the vigor with which you embrace recreational activities such as this, Mr. Rose.
jokes.setup: Moira has just established that singing is only Johnny's hobby, not his profession
jokes.punchline: 'Recreational activities such as this' frames singing in a community choir as equivalent to, say, model trains — a hobby for normal people. Moira's formal register makes it both insulting and affectionate.
jokes.explanation: Another perfect Moira diminishment delivered with full warmth. 'Recreational activities such as this' is beautifully condescending.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, that's nice. Patrick sent us a message.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Of course he did.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It says, 'Thinking of you both on your special day. Wishing you a lifetime of happiness together.'

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's very sweet.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He also says he misses me and can't wait until I get back.

jokes.text: Ooh, Okay ladies, if you don't mind, I'm going to allow my husband to steal me away, for a modest repast across the avenue.
jokes.setup: The group is wrapping up rehearsal and making post-rehearsal plans
jokes.punchline: 'A modest repast across the avenue' = dinner at the café across the street. Moira's baroque language transforms the mundane into the grandiose — and 'modest' is the self-aware twist.
jokes.explanation: Moira's verbal extravagance applied to the humblest possible activity. 'Across the avenue' for what is clearly just across the street is a perfect Moira-ism.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: And these are our lovebird wings.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: What kind of bird?

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: Chicken.

jokes.text: Boy, we never did this back in the Rose Video days, did we? / No, but I'm sure we had our reasons.
jokes.setup: Johnny reflects warmly on how they now socialize with the Jazzagals after rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'No, but I'm sure we had our reasons' is a masterpiece of implication — they clearly didn't mingle with people like this when they were wealthy, and she's too tactful (barely) to say so outright
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest lines. 'I'm sure we had our reasons' is the perfect Moira-coded snobbery — it's devastatingly honest while maintaining plausible deniability. Maximum economy, high originality in how it implies class consciousness without stating it. Justifies 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I was dating this guy in Monaco, and he was really into competitive sailing. So I learned how to sail. Then he dumped me for his sailing instructor, which was humiliating. But then I dated the sailing instructor, and he taught me everything he knew. Then *he* dumped me for a Prince, which, you know, you can't really compete with that. But the Prince had a yacht, so I spent three summers on the Mediterranean, and I basically became a professional sailor by default.

jokes.text: I didn't recognize you without your nipples. / Huh? / The puppies... the um, the cute little puppies that you love to photograph yourself with.
jokes.setup: Alexis meets Miguel in person for the first time and tries to reference his profile photo
jokes.punchline: She accidentally leads with 'nipples' — the detail David kept referencing — then panics and tries to redirect to the puppies. The scramble makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding callback that turns David's earlier nipple fixation into Alexis's awkward first impression. 'I didn't recognize you without your nipples' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. The structure is perfect: the blunder → confusion → failed correction. Justifies 8+ as an expertly constructed callback with high quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Miguel
dialogue.line: I'm nervous about this.

dialogue.speaker: Miguel
dialogue.line: Wait, why did I just say that out loud?

jokes.text: Brought the nipples. / Oh good. / Not about the nipples, um, about the puppies.
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to recover from the nipple comment
jokes.punchline: Miguel plays along with 'brought the nipples,' Alexis says 'Oh good,' then immediately reverses to clarify she meant the puppies — the correction is worse than the original blunder
jokes.explanation: The extension of the nipple awkwardness — Miguel's game 'brought the nipples', Alexis's auto-agreement 'oh good', the horror of what she just said — is a well-sustained cringe escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, right? I don't know what I was worried about.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Actually, um, there is something I need to tell you.

jokes.text: Uh, they serve champagne here? / I believe it's called Zhampagne, it's from the very famous Zhampagne region of France.
jokes.setup: Miguel offers champagne at what seems like a humble restaurant
jokes.punchline: Alexis covers for the restaurant's pretension by inventing 'Zhampagne' from the 'Zhampagne region' — a completely fabricated geography delivered with full confidence
jokes.explanation: The 'Zhampagne' bit is a lovely piece of Alexis-specific comedy — she would absolutely invent a fancy provenance to avoid admitting something is cheap. The confident delivery of 'very famous Zhampagne region' is inspired. Will be a fan-favorite line.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We're not really together, we're just... seeing each other.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, not seeing each other, we're more like... in contact.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, we text. Sporadically.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He texts. I sometimes read them.

jokes.text: Hm, you learn pretty quickly when you're in a Ugandan diamond smuggler's villa playing for your friend's freedom. / Right, right. Wait, what? / That was fun.
jokes.setup: David asks how Stevie got so good at... something (likely a spa treatment or game)
jokes.punchline: Stevie's casual reference to 'a Ugandan diamond smuggler's villa playing for your friend's freedom' is completely insane and delivered without any emphasis — the absurdity of the content versus the flatness of the delivery is the joke
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single best joke. The specificity — 'Ugandan diamond smuggler's villa', 'playing for your friend's freedom' — is wildly original. The deadpan delivery ('That was fun') is the perfect button. It reveals Stevie has an insane backstory she's never mentioned, told with complete nonchalance. This easily justifies 8+ by being genuinely surprising, character-specific (Stevie's dry delivery makes it), and one of the most quotable lines in Schitt's Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Miguel
dialogue.line: Look, I'll be honest with you. When I found out Alexis and Ted had a history, part of me wanted to use that against him in court. But then I realized that would make me a terrible person. So instead, I'm just going to be a mediocre lawyer and let the facts speak for themselves.

jokes.text: I actually changed my shirt 3 times for tonight and uh, I'm sorry, I probably shouldn't be giving that all away.
jokes.setup: Miguel is trying to express that there's something special about Alexis
jokes.punchline: He reveals the vulnerable detail of changing his shirt three times, then catches himself — the self-consciousness is endearing and funny
jokes.explanation: Relatable awkward-date comedy. Works because Miguel is a good sport and the self-awareness is charming. Not exceptional but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I was going to dump you anyway, so joke's on you.

jokes.text: I used to date Ted. / I'm sorry? / Ted Mullins? He's the veterinarian.
jokes.setup: The date is going well; Alexis decides to be honest about something 'random'
jokes.punchline: She drops 'I used to date Ted' — Miguel's professional rival — out of nowhere, destroying the romantic atmosphere
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat — Alexis's honesty is exactly wrong at exactly the right moment. The timing of the revelation is well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh Johnny, welcome to the choir! We're so excited to have you. You know what they say — many hands make light work, and many voices make... light... singing?

jokes.text: Wait, weren't you his receptionist? / Executive Assistant. And yes, but only for like a minute.
jokes.setup: Miguel processes that Alexis dated Ted
jokes.punchline: Miguel's mislabeling of her job title ('receptionist') and Alexis's dignified correction ('Executive Assistant') while simultaneously admitting it only lasted 'a minute' is a perfect comic exchange
jokes.explanation: The dignity applied to a job she barely did is a good Alexis beat. Her priorities are always slightly off.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The choir is my life now. It's the only thing that fills the void. A void so vast and so deep that if you dropped a stone into it, you would never hear it land. A void that echoes with the sound of my own heartbeat, the only companion in the darkness of my existence.

jokes.text: But you guys were engaged? / Um, very briefly. / Uh-huh. / And just twice.
jokes.setup: Miguel is increasingly alarmed by the Ted revelations
jokes.punchline: 'Just twice' — being engaged twice to the same person is already unusual; delivering it as a qualifier ('just') implies there might have been a third time that was 'serious'
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. 'Very briefly. And just twice.' — the escalating qualifier that treats 'twice engaged to the same person' as a minor footnote is a masterclass in comedic escalation and character voice. The 'just' is doing enormous work, implying both that twice isn't that many AND that there might have been more. Economy is near-perfect. Justifies 8+ convincingly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My pleasures are few and simple: a good bottle of wine, a well-crafted cocktail, and watching my husband struggle with basic technology.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And of course, the occasional dinner party where I can pretend to be interested in other people's problems while mentally redecorating their homes.

jokes.text: There is nobody who wants to see Ted fail more than I do, professionally speaking. Personally, I feel like this is off limits.
jokes.setup: Alexis explains her and Ted's relationship history as context
jokes.punchline: Miguel's revelation that he actively wants Ted to fail professionally — but draws the line at dating Ted's ex on personal grounds — is a funny inversion of expected ethics
jokes.explanation: Good reversal — Miguel has exactly the opposite ethics from what you'd expect. He's fine with professional sabotage but scrupulous about personal matters. The specificity makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I should take my leave. Ronnie's scatological observations have become rather... indelicate.

jokes.text: It just feels... wrong. / Okay, that is what I've been saying, to literally everyone.
jokes.setup: Both characters reach the mutual conclusion they shouldn't date
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'that is what I've been saying, to literally everyone' reveals she's been workshopping this decision with the entire town
jokes.explanation: A good character beat — Alexis has no filter on who she shares private information with. 'Literally everyone' is the right word.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because I have spent the last fifteen years constructing an incredibly elaborate defense mechanism, and the moment that I let someone in, I panic, and I sabotage it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So, no, I didn't discuss my dating history because I was too busy trying to convince myself that I didn't care about him in the first place.

jokes.text: What, you've been telling people about this? / No. Some people. Just close friends.
jokes.setup: Miguel is alarmed that Alexis has told people about their potential date
jokes.punchline: The three-step walk-back: 'No. Some people. Just close friends.' — each step reveals more while trying to minimize
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation in three tight steps. Alexis's inability to stop herself from revealing more while trying to reassure him is very funny and very her. The economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I love you more than I've ever loved anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: How would you know?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I... that's fair.

jokes.text: I don't think I can do this. / Okay, just to be clear, I couldn't do this first.
jokes.setup: Both agree they can't pursue this
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately competes to have been the first to not want to do this — even in backing out of a date, she needs to 'win'
jokes.explanation: This is a great character joke. Even in rejection, Alexis competes. 'Just to be clear, I couldn't do this first' is a brilliantly Alexis-specific line — she needs credit for the decision she didn't even make first. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I've never had a best friend either.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah. I think we're both just too difficult.

jokes.text: So I guess I am paying for the Zhampagne then?
jokes.setup: The date has imploded; they're awkwardly wrapping up
jokes.punchline: Miguel's resigned acceptance that he's covering the bill — specifically calling back to 'Zhampagne' — is a perfect deflating button on the whole scene
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that earns more laughs by bringing back the Zhampagne gag at the moment of maximum defeat. The resigned economy of 'I guess I am paying' is perfect. Callbacks that add meaning to the original joke are always well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's depressing.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It really is.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I suppose we could be each other's best friends.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'd like that.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, don't get too excited. We're both extremely closed off and emotionally unavailable.

jokes.text: And so, he says to the cop, 'Well, if it was mine, I wouldn't've put it in my mouth.'
jokes.setup: Veronica is telling a joke at the post-rehearsal gathering
jokes.punchline: The punchline implies the joke involved something illicit in someone's mouth — context suggests it's a sexual or drug-related joke, delivered as a punchline we arrive at mid-story
jokes.explanation: We only hear the punchline of Veronica's joke, which is sufficient to understand the double entendre. It works as background texture but we don't know the full setup, limiting its impact.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's sad.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, what's sadder is that I ate it alone in my car.

jokes.text: Oh, Veronica, once again, you reign supreme as the queen of the triple entendres.
jokes.setup: Veronica has told a dirty joke with multiple layers
jokes.punchline: 'Triple entendres' — Moira's escalation of 'double entendre' to 'triple' is a great Moira-ism that's both absurd and somehow exactly right for Veronica's joke
jokes.explanation: 'Triple entendres' is a genuinely funny coinage — it extends the logic of 'double entendre' in a direction that makes a kind of sense while being absurd. Moira's formal compliment structure ('reign supreme as queen of') elevates it further.

jokes.text: I just have to say this. I know that we are the Jazzagals, but it was pretty great to have a Jazza-guy in our midst today.
jokes.setup: The group is reflecting on having Johnny join the rehearsal
jokes.punchline: 'Jazza-guy' — the masculine extension of 'Jazzagals' — is a simple but effective portmanteau that works as a warm callback to the episode's title
jokes.explanation: Warm and functional. 'Jazza-guy' is the episode's title punchline and it lands gently. Not a sharp joke but a satisfying payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, here's a promising one. 'Looking for someone who's DTF.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that means 'down to f—'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Down to... Foxtrot? Is that a dance thing? Because I was actually quite good at the Foxtrot.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's not a dance, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's disappointing. I was already picturing myself in a sequined gown.

jokes.text: Lemonades for Moira and me... / And two cosmos for the baritone section. / ♪ Yes, please ♪
jokes.setup: The group is ordering drinks; Ronnie volunteers for the next round
jokes.punchline: Johnny orders 'two cosmos for the baritone section' (i.e., himself and another man), then sings his 'yes please' — he's already adopted the musical affectation of the group
jokes.explanation: Johnny singing 'yes please' is a nice callback to his earlier inadvertent singing. The 'baritone section' is a funny self-designation. Warm and enjoyable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, what is this? Polygamy? I'm not interested in sharing my husband with multiple wives. That's not how I was raised.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that's not what it means.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, whatever it is, I'm swiping left. Oh no... oh no, no, no. Did I just swipe right on someone?

jokes.text: Oh, Moira. / My time with the Jazzagals.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks what Moira takes for granted in this town, expecting an emotional answer
jokes.punchline: The pause before the reveal ('Oh, Moira') suggests something profound; the answer — the Jazzagals — is genuinely sweet but also absurd given how much Moira resents the warmup exercises
jokes.explanation: The humor is gentle — Moira's attachment to the Jazzagals, despite her complaints, is touching and a little funny. More warm than comedic, but qualifies.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Ray, these photos are certainly... something. But I have to say, I'm far more interested in that vanity lighting in the background. Is that brushed nickel? The way it frames your face is almost secondary to how it illuminates what appears to be a very well-appointed powder room.

jokes.text: As sad as it sounds, these rehearsals have become a tiny oasis in the echo-y canyon that I now call my social life.
jokes.setup: Moira confesses what Jazzagals means to her
jokes.punchline: 'The echo-y canyon that I now call my social life' is simultaneously a beautiful piece of self-aware melancholy and a deliciously over-written metaphor — Moira's theatrical language applied to genuine loneliness
jokes.explanation: 'Echo-y canyon that I now call my social life' is a standout Moira line — it's theatrical to the point of absurdity while also being genuinely poignant. The word 'echo-y' in particular is perfect: it's colloquial where you'd expect grandeur, which makes the whole metaphor funnier and sadder. This is the show's signature blend of comedy and character depth working perfectly.

jokes.text: My nightly sleeps, afternoon naps, and of course meals with my husband.
jokes.setup: Moira lists the few pleasures keeping her going in Schitt's Creek
jokes.punchline: 'My nightly sleeps, afternoon naps' — that two of her three listed pleasures are forms of sleep reveals the depth of her withdrawal and ennui. 'Nightly sleeps' is a strangely formal way to say 'sleeping.'
jokes.explanation: The specificity of distinguishing 'nightly sleeps' from 'afternoon naps' as separate pleasures is quietly devastating and funny. Moira's dissolution is rendered with both pathos and comedy. 'Nightly sleeps' as a term is a great Moira-ism.

jokes.text: Oh god, no, we'll leave together, please. There are only so many ways I can feign amusement at Ronnie's comedia Del commode.
jokes.setup: Johnny offers to leave Moira to enjoy the post-rehearsal drinks alone
jokes.punchline: 'Comedia del commode' — Moira's coinage for Ronnie's dirty/toilet humor, riffing on 'Commedia dell'arte' — is an inspired Moira-ism that reveals she's been suffering through Ronnie's jokes all evening
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. 'Comedia del commode' is a brilliant piece of Moira wordplay — it references the Italian theatrical tradition and pivots to bathroom humor with perfect economy. Only Moira would know 'Commedia dell'arte' and deploy it as an insult about toilet jokes. Maximum character integration. Justifies 8+ emphatically.

jokes.text: So the lover's curry was a mistake. / Yeah, it was.
jokes.setup: Post-dinner reflection in the honeymoon suite
jokes.punchline: The shared flat acknowledgment that something called 'lover's curry' was a mistake is a perfect deadpan two-hander — the name alone tells you why
jokes.explanation: Efficient and funny. 'Lover's curry' is a ridiculous restaurant offering, and the shared 'yeah, it was' is a nice moment of David-Stevie partnership humor.

jokes.text: Patrick would find it funny. / We will not be telling Patrick.
jokes.setup: David imagines Patrick's reaction to the curry incident
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat veto — 'We will not be telling Patrick' — closes the exchange, implying the story is in fact so embarrassing it can't leave this room despite David's instinct to share it with Patrick
jokes.explanation: Good button on the curry sequence. Stevie's flat 'We will not be telling Patrick' has nice finality. The joke is the firmness of the veto.

jokes.text: Uh, but we got through it, and now we have a story we can only tell each other so... / Patrick would find it funny. / We will not be telling Patrick. / Another secret for the pile.
jokes.setup: Stevie tries to find a silver lining in the disastrous curry experience
jokes.punchline: David's 'Another secret for the pile' reveals they have accumulated a concerning number of secrets, which is both funny and a genuine character observation about how complicated their current situation is
jokes.explanation: 'Another secret for the pile' is a great line that's funny and also quietly reveals something true about their friendship. The pile is evidently large.

jokes.text: I guess I just thought the deeper we got into his past, the deeper we'd have to get into mine. And, historically speaking, the more I revealed of myself, the less interested people got.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks why David didn't ask Patrick about his dating history
jokes.punchline: 'The more I revealed of myself, the less interested people got' is a sad/funny observation — it's darkly comic because of how matter-of-factly David states it as a historical pattern
jokes.explanation: This is comedy that earns genuine pathos. The 'historically speaking' qualifier makes it both analytical and funny — David has apparently run this experiment multiple times. A moment where comedy and character revelation merge perfectly.

jokes.text: I think you're my best friend. / You think? / Well, I can't know for sure, because I'm realizing now that I don't think I've ever really had one.
jokes.setup: David makes a heartfelt declaration to Stevie
jokes.punchline: He immediately qualifies it — he can't confirm she's his best friend because he's not sure he's ever had one to compare to — which is both touching and very funny
jokes.explanation: This is very well done — the warmth of the moment is preserved while the comedy of 'I think' / 'I can't know for sure' subverts it gently. It's a funny line that also reveals genuine character depth. The 8+ is justified because it's doing double duty: genuine comedy AND emotional resonance, which is the hardest thing to pull off.

jokes.text: This would be a really sweet moment, if what we had just admitted to each other wasn't so sad.
jokes.setup: Both David and Stevie have admitted they've never had a best friend before
jokes.punchline: David narrates the scene's own emotional register — acknowledging both the sweetness and the sadness simultaneously — which is a meta move that deflates and elevates at once
jokes.explanation: This is sophisticated comedy writing. David's ability to hold both 'sweet' and 'sad' simultaneously while narrating the scene's own emotional content is very meta and very David. It's funny because it's true, and touching because the characters know it too. Economy is excellent. Easily 8+.

jokes.text: Sadder? When you were in the bathroom, I finished the rest of the lover's curry.
jokes.setup: David has just said the moment is 'really sad'
jokes.punchline: Stevie one-ups the sadness by revealing she secretly ate the rest of the lover's curry they'd agreed was a mistake — which is both gross and deeply human
jokes.explanation: This is beautifully constructed. The callback to the lover's curry — which they mutually agreed was terrible — being secretly finished by Stevie is a perfect subversion. It's a three-layered joke: (1) the callback itself, (2) the hypocrisy of Stevie secretly eating it, and (3) the fact that this is somehow sadder than having no best friends. Justifies 8+ easily — masterful callback execution.

jokes.text: Okay, you'll be sleeping in the bathtub tonight. / I understand.
jokes.setup: Stevie has been caught eating the secret curry
jokes.punchline: David issues a punishment ('bathtub') and Stevie accepts it completely without protest — 'I understand' is the perfectly submissive response that shows she knows she deserves it
jokes.explanation: 'I understand' with full acceptance is the perfect button. Stevie's complete capitulation — no argument, no negotiation — is funnier than any pushback would have been.

jokes.text: Well, honey, I don't think you should let one embarrassing encounter color your experience, on Bumper. / Bump-kin.
jokes.setup: Johnny gives Alexis advice about online dating
jokes.punchline: Johnny calls it 'Bumper' — another wrong version of the app name — and Alexis corrects him with the patient weariness of someone who has done this many times
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag on the app name. Each wrong version is a little different which is a nice touch. Not the freshest joke but consistent.

jokes.text: So you upload your photo, and the Bumpkin sends it to local singles. / It's not called The Bumpkin.
jokes.setup: Johnny continues his Bumpkin misunderstanding, now adding 'The' as if it's an institution
jokes.punchline: 'The Bumpkin' — Johnny's addition of a definite article makes it sound like a local pub or institution, which is even more wrong than just 'Bumper'
jokes.explanation: Each iteration of the name gag adds a small escalation. 'The Bumpkin' is a funny evolution — it makes the app sound like a country tavern.

jokes.text: Though harvesting crops does produce a nicely-sculpted physique.
jokes.setup: Moira is looking at a farmer's profile on Bumpkin
jokes.punchline: Moira's deadpan agricultural analysis of Isaac's physique — phrased as an academic observation — is both funny in its detachment and revealing of her actual interest
jokes.explanation: Moira's formal academic framing of what is clearly appreciation for a hot farmer is a lovely character beat. 'Nicely-sculpted physique' in the context of 'harvesting crops' is perfectly Moira.

jokes.text: 'Looking for a hook-up.' / Well, that sounds promising, Alexis, he's open for hooking up for dinner, or drinks.
jokes.setup: Isaac's profile says he's 'looking for a hook-up' in the dating app slang sense
jokes.punchline: Johnny interprets 'hook-up' literally as meeting for a casual social occasion — his naivety about dating app terminology is the joke
jokes.explanation: Classic generational/cultural gap joke. Johnny's earnest misreading is charming. Not original (older person misreads slang is a standard joke) but executed with appropriate lightness.

jokes.text: Comes from a large family. Oh, not my kids.
jokes.setup: Moira reads about Jebediah's large family as a potential positive
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, not my kids' — Moira immediately rules out having children with this potential match, which reveals she was considering him seriously enough to think about family planning
jokes.explanation: Very funny Moira line. The dark implication — she's ruling out having his children before ruling out the man — combined with the slight on her existing children ('not my kids') is sharp and very in character.

jokes.text: Oh, Alexis, look, you've just Bumpkined with Jebediah. / What? Ew!
jokes.setup: Moira is swiping through Alexis's phone on the app
jokes.punchline: Moira coins 'Bumpkined' as a verb — accidentally matching with Jebediah — which is both a funny invention and a callback to the name jokes throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: Moira verbifying 'Bumpkin' is a lovely piece of linguistic comedy that also serves as a callback. 'You've just Bumpkined with Jebediah' is a great absurdist sentence.

jokes.text: And now we're back to Ray. / Ew! / How did Ray get such a beautiful bathroom?
jokes.setup: The Bumpkin misadventure cycles back to Ray's multiple profiles
jokes.punchline: Ray's profiles keep appearing; Johnny's non-sequitur question — 'How did Ray get such a beautiful bathroom?' — indicates Ray's profile photo shows him in a nice bathroom, and this is what Johnny's mind fixates on
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent ending joke. The callback to Ray's multiple profiles is satisfying, but the button — Johnny's completely left-field concern about Ray's bathroom — is the real punch. It reframes Ray as having unexpectedly nice real estate, and Johnny's bewilderment about this minor detail is a perfect non-sequitur button for the episode's final scene. Justifies 8+.

tmdb_overview: After the barbecue fiasco, Stevie takes David to a spa for some consoling, but she bought a honeymoon package making things worse. Johnny impresses the Jazzagals with his talents, but Moira doesn't like his joining the group. Alexis tries out a dating app and dates a cute local.
insight: David's jazz bar venture crumbles under 59 mostly deadpan jokes—character comedy at its sharpest.
